title: The Turkic Era on the Territory of Kazakhstan (6th–12th centuries)
overview: Lecture 2 traces six centuries of Turkic statehood on the territory of Kazakhstan: the rise of the unified Turkic Khaganate under Bumin and Istemi (552), its split into Eastern and Western Khaganates (603), the Türgesh resistance to the Arabs and Tang Chinese (Battle of Talas 751), the Karluks, Oghuz, Kimak and Kipchak confederations, and the Karakhanid dynasty (942) – the first Turkic Islamic state. The era forged Turkic identity, the runic script, control of the Silk Road, and the eventual Islamization of Central Asia.

coreConcepts.definition: Early-feudal nomadic state ruled by a khagan (great khan). Multi-tiered hierarchy with titles such as Yabghu, Shad, Elteber, Buyuruq and Tarhan; commoners called 'kara budun' (black folk).
coreConcepts.example: Turkic Khaganate (552–603), Western Turkic Khaganate (603–704), Türgesh, Karluk, Karakhanid.

coreConcepts.definition: Ruling Turkic clan whose origin myth (recorded in Chinese 'Zhou shu' and 'Sui shu') traces back to a boy nursed by a she-wolf who fathered ten sons – progenitors of the Turks.
coreConcepts.example: Sergey Klyashtorny interpreted the legend as memory of Xiongnu remnant tribes migrating from the Nan Mountains to the Altai by 460 CE.

coreConcepts.definition: Trans-Eurasian trade network linking China, Central Asia, Persia and Byzantium; passed through Zhetysu, Sogdiana, Ferghana, Khwarazm, Kashgar, Shash and Tokharistan, all under Turkic control.
coreConcepts.example: Sogdian merchants were the principal traders, working in symbiosis with the Turkic khagans.

coreConcepts.definition: Pre-Islamic Turkic religion centered on the sky-god Tengri, the earth-goddess Umai, and ancestor spirits. Coexisted with shamanism, Manichaeism and Buddhism.
coreConcepts.example: Orkhon runic inscriptions (8th c.) address Tengri directly.

coreConcepts.definition: Administrative system of 20 districts (tutuks), each fielding 7,000 warriors, under Türgesh Khagan Uch-Elik.
coreConcepts.example: Capital at Suyab on the Chu River; second capital at Kungut on the Ili River.

coreConcepts.definition: Decisive engagement between the Arab Abbasid Caliphate and Tang China. The Karluks switched to the Arab side mid-battle, ensuring an Arab victory.
coreConcepts.example: Tang Empire withdrew from Zhetysu and Eastern Turkestan; the Arabs also retreated to Shash. The battle opened Central Asia to Islam.

coreConcepts.definition: First Turkic dynasty to officially adopt Islam; combined Turkic-nomadic political culture with Islamic urban civilization. Founded by Satuk Bughra Khan, grandson of a Karluk khagan.
coreConcepts.example: In 960 some 200,000 Turks in Zhetysu converted to Islam under Musa, the son of Satuk Bughra Khan.

expandedNotes.heading: Origins of the Turks
expandedNotes.body: Chinese chronicles ('Zhou shu' 周书, 'Sui shu' 隋史, 'Bei shi' 北史) describe the Turks as a branch of the late Xiongnu. After their original state was destroyed, a single boy survived; a she-wolf rescued and nursed him, then took refuge in a cave where she bore ten sons – the youngest, Ashina, became eponymous ancestor of the ruling clan. Klyashtorny reads the legend as ethnographic memory of Xiongnu groups who migrated to the Nan Mountains in the late 3rd century, then to Turpan in the late 4th, and were forced by the Rouran (Avars) into the Altai by 460 CE. There the Ashina mixed with Sogdian (Iranian) and Tocharian (Indo-European) peoples – contacts that shaped their administrative practices and language.

expandedNotes.heading: Rise of Bumin and Istemi (542–552)
expandedNotes.body: In the Altai, the Ashina paid iron tribute to the Rouran Khaganate. War in northern China between Eastern and Western Wei opened a diplomatic opening; in 545 a Wei envoy reached Bumin, and the Turks shifted allegiance. To pretext a revolt, Bumin demanded a Rouran princess in marriage; insulted ('You are my blacksmith slave'), the Rouran khan Anagui's refusal allowed Bumin to ally with Western Wei (princess Changle, 551) and crush the Rouran in 552. Bumin took the title Il-Khan but died that year. His successors Kara-Qaghan and Mughan-Qaghan finished off the Rouran – some fled west, becoming the Avars who raided Byzantium from the Danube by 558–568.

expandedNotes.heading: Mughan Qaghan and Istemi (553–576)
expandedNotes.body: Mughan Qaghan (553–572) extended Turkic rule from Korea to the Caspian and from the Gobi to Lake Baikal, subjugating the Khitan and Yenisei Kyrgyz and forcing Northern Qi and Northern Zhou to pay tribute. Istemi, Bumin's younger brother, allied with Sasanian Iran (cementing the alliance through marriage of Shah Khosrow I to Istemi's daughter) to destroy the Hephthalite empire (560–565), splitting Hephthalite lands between the Turks and Iran. When Iran refused to let Sogdian silk merchants cross to Byzantium (Khosrow Anushirvan reportedly bought Turkic silk and burned it in front of envoys), Istemi sent the Sogdian Maniah to Constantinople; Emperor Justin II reciprocated with the envoy Zemarchus (568).

expandedNotes.heading: Crisis and Split of 603
expandedNotes.body: After the death of Khagan Taspar in 581, succession disputes split the ruling clan and the Sui dynasty exploited the chaos with several campaigns. After 20+ years of civil war, in 603 the empire formally divided into Eastern (Mongolia) and Western (Central Asia and Kazakhstan) Turkic Khaganates.

expandedNotes.heading: Western Turkic Khaganate (603–704) – 'On Ok El'
expandedNotes.body: Known as 'On Ok El' – the 'State of the Ten Arrows' – reflecting governance by ten tribal unions. Capital at Suya (Suyab) on the Chu River (the ruins of Ak-Beshim near modern Tokmak, Kyrgyzstan); summer residence at Ming-Bulak near Isfijab. Apex under Shegui (611–618) and Tong Yabghu (618–630): borders pushed to the Amu Darya and Hindu Kush. Tong Yabghu personally joined Byzantine Emperor Heraclius's third Transcaucasian campaign (627–628), taking Chor (Derbent) and meeting Heraclius at the walls of Tbilisi, where the emperor placed his own crown on the Khagan's head and offered his daughter Princess Eudocia. After Tong Yabghu's assassination civil war (630–657) opened the door to the Tang Empire; in 659 the captured khagan died.

expandedNotes.heading: Türgesh Khaganate (704–756)
expandedNotes.body: Restored Turkic statehood under Uch-Elik (699–706), who expelled the Tang puppet khan and divided the country into 20 administrative districts (tutuks) of 7,000 warriors each. Main capital Suyab, second capital Kungut. Wars with the Arab Caliphate began in 705 (Arab governor Qutayba ibn Muslim took Bukhara in 706 and 709). Internal struggles continued under Sakal Khagan (706–711). Revival under Suluk Khagan (715–738), known to Arabs as Abu Muzahim ('the Troublemaker'). The black-Türgesh capital moved to Talas (Taraz). Suluk fought on two fronts (Arabs in the west, Tang-backed claimants in the east); defeated the Arabs in 723 with the men of Shash; defeated again in 732 and killed by his own general Baga Tarkhan in 737. An Arab envoy's record (preserved by al-Faqih) describes the khagan dismissing Islam – 'Among my warriors there are no bath-house workers, shoemakers or tailors' – while parading 10,000-strong banner formations of cavalry.

expandedNotes.heading: Battle of Atlakh / Talas (751 CE) and the Karluks
expandedNotes.body: After Suluk's death, struggle between 'yellow' and 'black' Türgesh weakened the khaganate. In 746 Karluks migrated to Zhetysu from the Altai and Tarbagatai. In 748 the Tang Empire took Suyab and executed the ruler of Shash. In 751 at Atlakh (Talas) the Arab Abbasid army met the Tang. The Karluks switched to the Arab side mid-battle, delivering victory; the Tang withdrew permanently from Zhetysu and Eastern Turkestan, and the Arabs also retreated to Shash. The Türgesh Khaganate collapsed in 756, replaced by the Karluk Khaganate.

expandedNotes.heading: Karluk Khaganate (756–940)
expandedNotes.body: Karluks: ancient Turkic tribe from the Altai region. In 744 they joined the Nine Oghuz rebellion against the Second Eastern Turkic Khaganate; defeated in 746 by the Uyghurs and pushed to Zhetysu between the Ili and Chu. By 756 they controlled Zhetysu, modern southern Kazakhstan and parts of Kyrgyzstan. Capital Suyab on the Chu; second capital Kungut on the Ili. When the Uyghur Khaganate collapsed under Kyrgyz pressure in 840, the Karluk yabgu proclaimed himself khagan. Continuous resistance to Arab expansion. Decline began as their own Islamic Karakhanid offshoot rose to power around 940.

expandedNotes.heading: Oghuz, Kimak and Kipchak
expandedNotes.body: OGHUZ: Confederation in the lower Syr Darya and around the Aral Sea (9th–11th c.). Loose union of tribes under a yabgu; capital Yangikent. In the 11th century one Oghuz branch – the Seljuks – converted to Islam and founded the Seljuk Empire in the Middle East. KIMAK: Confederation in the Irtysh basin (8th–11th c.), part of the broader Kimak-Kipchak union; capital Kimakia; renowned cavalry. KIPCHAK: Turkic group ('Cumans' to Europeans, 'Polovtsy' to Rus chroniclers), descendants of the Kimaks. From the 10th–11th centuries they migrated west, dominating the steppes from the Urals to the Danube – the vast 'Dasht-i Kipchak' ('Kipchak Steppe'). They served as mercenaries for Rus, Byzantium and others; ultimately absorbed by the Mongol Empire after the 1219–1224 conquest.

expandedNotes.heading: Karakhanid Dynasty (942–1212)
expandedNotes.body: Founded from the Karluk elite. Satuk Bughra Khan, grandson of a Karluk khagan, secretly converted to Islam and, with the help of Muslim Turks from Kashgar and the Samanid dynasty, overthrew his uncle. Officially formed 942; in 999 Karakhanids took Transoxiana (Maverannahr) from the Samanids. ISLAMIZATION: According to Arab geographer Ibn Hawqal, thousands of Turkic families between Isfijab and Shash converted in the 10th century. Musa, who inherited the throne in 955, declared Islam the state religion; in 960 some 200,000 Turks in Zhetysu accepted Islam.

expandedNotes.heading: Karakhanid Governance and Culture
expandedNotes.body: Dual-power system between two tribal groups – Chigils and Yagma. Eastern (senior) khan – Arslan Kara-Khakan – from the Chigils, capitals at Kashgar and Balasagun (the Burana archaeological site near Tokmak, Kyrgyzstan). Western (junior) khan – Bogra Kara-Khakan – from the Yagma, capital at Taraz, later Samarkand. Complex hierarchy of ilek, tegin and other princely titles. Mosques, madrasas; mausoleums of Aisha Bibi, Babadzha Khatun and Karakhan still stand. Yusuf Balasaguni wrote 'Kutadgu Bilig' (1069), the first major Turkic literary work. Mahmud Kashgari compiled the encyclopedic Turkic-language dictionary 'Diwan Lughat al-Turk' (1072–1074). Abu Nasr al-Farabi (870–950), a native of Otrar, was called 'the Second Teacher' after Aristotle and authored hundreds of philosophical and scientific treatises.

expandedNotes.heading: Kara-Khitan Invasion and Decline (1125–1212)
expandedNotes.body: Kara-Khitans (Western Liao) arrived from the east in 1125, were initially accepted into Karakhanid service, then revolted in 1128 and captured Balasagun. In 1141 the Karakhanids were crushed at the Battle of Qatwan and reduced to Kara-Khitan vassals. In the early 13th century the Naiman ruler Kuchlug captured the Kara-Khitan ruler. The last Karakhanid in Transoxiana was killed by Muhammad Khwarezmshah in 1212. Soon after, the Mongol invasion of 1219 ended the entire era.

keyScholars.name: Bumin Qaghan
keyScholars.contribution: Founder of the Turkic Khaganate in 552; took the title Il-Khan.

keyScholars.name: Istemi Qaghan
keyScholars.contribution: Younger brother of Bumin; extended the Khaganate to the Caspian and Aral, allied with Sasanian Iran against the Hephthalites and with Byzantium against Iran; opened the Silk Road embassy track.

keyScholars.name: Mughan Qaghan
keyScholars.contribution: Subjugated Khitan and Yenisei Kyrgyz; forced Northern Qi and Zhou to pay tribute; Chinese chronicles say he 'struck fear into all territories beyond the Great Wall'.

keyScholars.name: Tong Yabghu Qaghan
keyScholars.contribution: Peak of Western Turkic Khaganate; joined Heraclius's Caucasus campaign; centralized tax collection via Tudun officials.

keyScholars.name: Uch-Elik Khagan
keyScholars.contribution: Founder of the Türgesh state; created the 20-tutuk 'Ten Arrows' administrative system.

keyScholars.name: Suluk Khagan
keyScholars.contribution: Greatest Türgesh ruler ('Abu Muzahim'); defeated Arabs at Shash (723); fought on two fronts; capital moved to Talas.

keyScholars.name: Satuk Bughra Khan
keyScholars.contribution: Founder of the Karakhanid dynasty; first Turkic ruler to officially adopt Islam (~942).

keyScholars.name: Musa b. Satuk
keyScholars.contribution: Karakhanid khan who declared Islam the state religion; in 960 about 200,000 Turks in Zhetysu accepted Islam.

keyScholars.name: Abu Nasr al-Farabi
keyScholars.contribution: Born in Otrar; philosopher and polymath ('Second Teacher' after Aristotle); wrote 'On the Virtuous City' and ~150 other works.

keyScholars.name: Mahmud Kashgari
keyScholars.contribution: Author of 'Diwan Lughat al-Turk' (1072–1074), the encyclopedic dictionary of Turkic languages.

keyScholars.name: Yusuf Balasaguni
keyScholars.contribution: Author of 'Kutadgu Bilig' (1069), the first major Turkic literary work in verse.

keyScholars.name: Sergey Klyashtorny
keyScholars.contribution: Soviet/Russian Turkologist who decoded the Orkhon runic inscriptions and reinterpreted the she-wolf origin myth.

realWorldExamples: The Burana Tower at the site of Balasagun (Kyrgyzstan) is the surviving Karakhanid minaret.
Mausoleums of Aisha Bibi (near Taraz), Babadzha Khatun and Karakhan are major architectural monuments of the Karakhanid era.
The Otrar oasis – birthplace of al-Farabi – is now an archaeological reserve and was a major Silk Road city until destroyed by the Mongols.
Mahmud Kashgari's 'Diwan Lughat al-Turk' is the world's first comparative dictionary of Turkic languages and a primary source for Kazakh historical linguistics.
The Battle of Talas (751) is often cited as the moment Islam entered Central Asia – the path that led directly to modern Kazakh Muslim identity.

timeline.event: Rouran force Ashina clan into the Altai
First Wei embassy to Bumin – beginning of the rebellion
Bumin allies with Western Wei (marries princess Changle)
Bumin destroys the Rouran and founds the Turkic Khaganate
Avars (refugee Rouran) reach the Danube
Turkic-Sasanian alliance against the Hephthalites
Decisive Turkic victory at Nesa (Karshi)
Sogdian envoy Maniah and Byzantine envoy Zemarchus exchange embassies
Death of Khagan Taspar; Sui campaigns begin
Final split into Eastern and Western Turkic Khaganates
Reign of Tong Yabghu – peak of Western Khaganate
Tong Yabghu joins Heraclius's Caucasus campaign
Tang Empire captures the last Western Turkic khagan
Uch-Elik founds the Türgesh Khaganate
Suluk Khagan defeats the Arabs at Shash
Suluk killed; Türgesh power begins to fade
Tang capture Suyab; execute the ruler of Shash
Battle of Atlakh (Talas) – Karluks side with Arabs against Tang
Karluk Khaganate established
Fall of the Uyghur Khaganate; Karluk yabgu proclaims himself khagan
Karakhanid dynasty officially formed under Satuk Bughra Khan
200,000 Turks in Zhetysu convert to Islam under Musa
Karakhanids take Transoxiana from the Samanids
Yusuf Balasaguni completes 'Kutadgu Bilig'
Mahmud Kashgari writes 'Diwan Lughat al-Turk'
Kara-Khitans capture Balasagun
Battle of Qatwan – Karakhanids become Kara-Khitan vassals
Last Karakhanid ruler killed by Muhammad Khwarezmshah

mustMemorize: 552 – Bumin Qaghan founds the Turkic Khaganate
603 – split into Eastern and Western Turkic Khaganates
Western Turkic Khaganate = 'On Ok El' (State of Ten Arrows); capital Suyab
751 – Battle of Talas (Atlakh): Arabs + Karluks defeat Tang China
942 – Karakhanid dynasty founded by Satuk Bughra Khan
960 – 200,000 Turks in Zhetysu accept Islam under Musa
1141 – Battle of Qatwan; 1212 – end of Karakhanids
cheatSheet:
Turkic (552) → Western Turkic (603, Suyab) → Türgesh (704, Talas capital) → Karluk (756, after Talas 751) → Karakhanid (942, Islam 960) → Kara-Khitan (1141 Qatwan) → Mongols (1219)
Key texts: Kashgari 'Diwan' 1072 + Balasaguni 'Kutadgu Bilig' 1069
Al-Farabi = Otrar = 'Second Teacher'
mnemonics: B-I-M-T-S-U – Bumin, Istemi, Mughan, Tong Yabghu, Suluk, Uch-Elik (chronology of key khagans)
751 = Talas Tipping (Karluks tip the battle to Arabs)
960 = NINE-SIX-ZERO – the year Zhetysu went Islamic
Kutadgu Bilig 1069 + Diwan 1072 – three years apart; remember as 'K before D'
